featured_promo.title: What I Learned at Fair Housing Day 2026 (and Why You Should Care)
featured_promo.body: Harv spent April 14 at UC Berkeley for the 5th Annual C.A.R. Fair Housing Day — seven sessions, three hundred REALTORS in Pauley Ballroom. He rebuilt economist Oscar Wei's 11-slide closing deck as interactive charts you can click through in about ten minutes.
featured_promo.quote: Black first-time buyers in California were 4 percent in 2010. In 2025, they are still 4 percent. Fifteen years of attention, zero movement at the entry point.
featured_promo.quote_attribution: — Oscar Wei, Deputy Chief Economist, C.A.R.

stocks.note: Numbers as of market close April 16th.
stocks.news:
S&P 500 and NASDAQ both printed fresh record highs this week as traders priced in an earlier end to the Iran conflict. Records make for nice headlines — but they don't change how a pullback feels, so stay disciplined.

A federal jury ruled Live Nation (LYV) ran an illegal ticket monopoly, siding with 36 states and D.C. The remedy could force a sell-off of venues and promotion arms — good for competition, bad for a stock built on control.

The SEC scrapped a 30-year-old rule that required retail day traders to hold $25,000 minimum balances. Robinhood (HOOD) popped 10% on the news as the sandbox just got a lot bigger. More access ≠ better returns — worth reminding clients who are feeling bold.

economy.note: US 10-Year and Gold as of 4:00pm ET April 16th; WTI Crude reflects today's intraday move.
economy.commentary:
WTI Crude took an 11% face-plant today after Iran's foreign minister said the Strait of Hormuz will stay open. Supply fears evaporated, and the premium baked into oil since the war began came right back out. Gasoline prices should follow in the days ahead — a quiet tailwind for household budgets.

Jobless claims dipped again last week — the labor market is still stuck in a "low hire, low fire" holding pattern. Snapchat is trimming 1,000 jobs (16% of staff), a reminder that "low fire" doesn't mean zero fire.

The U.S. Treasury put two Chinese banks on notice for allegedly helping Iran move money — sanctions could follow. Iran has reportedly been asking oil tankers to pay in crypto just to keep the lights on.

crypto.commentary:
Tether quietly added another $70 million in Bitcoin, lifting its reserves to 97,141 BTC (~$7.16 billion). That makes the stablecoin issuer the #2 corporate Bitcoin holder on the planet — second only to MicroStrategy's 780,897-BTC war chest.

South Korea is piloting bank-issued blockchain deposits across nine major banks later this year. Real money becomes programmable digital currency — spending rules (and fraud prevention) baked directly into the ledger. Watch the rest of Asia follow.

real_estate.homebuilder: Mortgage rates ticked slightly lower today — 30-year down 3 bps, 15-year down 5 bps. MBS pricing is firming up, which could mean a bit more room to run.
real_estate.commentary:
Total inventory across our 15-city tracker moved up to 2,717 actives — with 227 coming soon and 269 new listings hitting the market. The spring pipeline is still filling.

📍 Fremont: 337 total, 48 coming soon, 34 new — 44 townhomes active with 14 pending, 81 condos active with 14 pending, and 112 detached/duplex/PUD active with 72 pending. The detached side still drives most of the pending action.

📍 Oakland: 757 total, 29 coming soon, 51 new — still the Bay's deepest pool by a mile.

📍 Hayward: 223 total, 17 coming soon, 15 new — steady buyer interest.

📍 Livermore: 214 total, 8 coming soon, 27 new — fresh activity continues.

📍 San Ramon: 184 total, 19 coming soon, 25 new — pipeline staying full.

📍 Danville: 181 total, 8 coming soon, 31 new — a big jump in new listings.

📍 Dublin: 175 total, 19 coming soon, 28 new.

📍 Pleasanton: 134 total, 16 coming soon, 19 new.

📍 Milpitas: 115 total, 26 coming soon, 3 new — CS pipeline is loaded.

📍 Newark: 114 total, 9 coming soon, 6 new.

📍 San Leandro: 94 total, 5 coming soon, 10 new.

📍 Union City: 85 total, 11 coming soon, 4 new — still one of the tightest markets on the board.

Northern neighbors: Canada's housing market is forecast to grow just 1% in 2026, with the national median new-home price projected near $688,955. Higher oil-driven inflation has triggered rate-hike chatter right at peak buying season — sound familiar?

Build-to-rent retreat: Single-family build-to-rent starts fell 19% year-over-year — from 84,000 units in 2024 to 68,000 in 2025. Financing costs climbed while rents rose just 1.2%, so the math stopped penciling out for investors.
